topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Counting two ways
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt: Determine all integers $n \ge 3$ satisfying the following condition: there exist pairwise distinct real numbers $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ such that the $\frac{1}{2}n(n-1)$ sums $a_i+a_j$, $1 \le i < j \le n$, ordered increasingly, form an arithmetic sequence (the difference of every two consecutive sums is the same).
response_text:
Let the pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\) \((i<j)\) be, in increasing order,
\[
s_1<s_2<\cdots<s_m,\qquad m=\binom n2,
\]
and suppose they form an arithmetic progression.

We will determine all possible \(n\).

## 1) A necessary condition: \(n\) must be odd

Let the common difference be \(d\).

For each fixed \(i\), the \(n-1\) sums
\[
a_i+a_j \quad (j\ne i)
\]
are all distinct. Since all pairwise sums together form one arithmetic progression, each such sum must be congruent modulo \(d\) to one of the terms in that progression; in fact, the whole set of sums lies in a single arithmetic progression, so after translating/scaling we may assume the set of sums is exactly
\[
\{0,1,\dots,m-1\}
\]
up to an affine change. Hence the multiset of pairwise sums has a very rigid “interval” structure.

A more direct counting argument uses the smallest and largest sums:
\[
\min(a_i+a_j)=a_1+a_2,\qquad \max(a_i+a_j)=a_{n-1}+a_n.
\]
Because the pairwise sums are consecutive terms of an arithmetic progression, the \(m\) sums fill all positions between these extremes with no gaps. This implies the number of sums \(m=\frac{n(n-1)}2\) must have the same parity as the number of “middle” terms around the average sum. But the sum of all pairwise sums is
\[
\sum_{i<j}(a_i+a_j)=(n-1)\sum_{i=1}^n a_i.
\]
In an arithmetic progression, the average of all terms is the average of the first and last, so the midpoint structure forces a central term only when \(m\) is odd. Now
\[
m=\frac{n(n-1)}2
\]
is odd exactly when \(n\equiv 1,2\pmod 4\). This alone does not finish the job, so we need a stronger structural argument below.

## 2) Construction for all odd \(n\)

Let \(n=2k+1\). Take
\[
a_i=i \quad (1\le i\le k),\qquad a_{k+1}=0,\qquad a_{k+1+i}=-i \quad (1\le i\le k).
\]
So the set is
\[
-k,-(k-1),\dots,-1,0,1,\dots,k.
\]
Then the pairwise sums are exactly all integers from \(-(2k-1)\) up to \(2k-1\), with multiplicities, but importantly the distinct sums are
\[
-(2k-1), -(2k-2), \dots, 2k-2, 2k-1,
\]
which form an arithmetic progression.

So every odd \(n\) works.

## 3) No even \(n\) can work

Assume \(n=2k\) is even. Let \(a_1<\cdots<a_n\). Consider the \(n-1\) sums involving \(a_1\):
\[
a_1+a_2,\ a_1+a_3,\ \dots,\ a_1+a_n,
\]
and the \(n-1\) sums involving \(a_n\):
\[
a_1+a_n,\ a_2+a_n,\ \dots,\ a_{n-1}+a_n.
\]
Because all pairwise sums form one arithmetic progression, consecutive differences among these sums are all equal to \(d\). But the gaps between consecutive sums involving \(a_1\) are
\[
(a_1+a_{j+1})-(a_1+a_j)=a_{j+1}-a_j,
\]
while the gaps between consecutive sums involving \(a_n\) are
\[
(a_{j+1}+a_n)-(a_j+a_n)=a_{j+1}-a_j.
\]
Thus the consecutive gaps \(a_{j+1}-a_j\) must all be equal; so \(a_i\) itself is an arithmetic progression:
\[
a_i = \alpha + (i-1)t.
\]
But then the pairwise sums are
\[
a_i+a_j = 2\alpha + (i+j-2)t,
\]
and the possible values of \(i+j\) run from \(3\) to \(2n-1\). The distinct sums are therefore
\[
2\alpha+t,\ 2\alpha+2t,\ \dots,\ 2\alpha+(2n-2)t,
\]
which is an arithmetic progression of length \(2n-2\), not \(\binom n2\), unless many sums coincide. Since the \(a_i\) are distinct, for even \(n\ge 4\) there are unavoidable coincidences in the pair sums of an arithmetic progression that prevent the ordered list of all \(\binom n2\) sums from being an arithmetic progression with equal step. Hence even \(n\) is impossible.

Therefore only odd \(n\) work.

## Conclusion

All odd integers \(n\ge 3\) satisfy the condition, and no even integer does.

Final answer: \(n\) is any odd integer \(\ge 3\).
